title: Worked example: anchoring signed workflow history for external finality — is the HistorySignature exportable?
body:
Hi Dapr/Diagrid team — congrats on Verifiable Execution in 1.18. I went through the `historysigning` package in `durabletask-go` and built a small worked example, and I have one genuine design question.

Disclosure: I work on the Mycelium / argentum-core project (on-chain accountability for agents). The repo below is my own, not affiliated with Dapr or Diagrid.

What I built: took a `HistorySignature` produced with the real `durabletask-go` v0.12.1 signing library (local SVID, synthetic events — not the Dapr runtime; provenance noted in the repo), computed the `SignatureDigest` of the chain head, and anchored that 32-byte digest permissionlessly on a public ledger (Base). An independent verifier recomputes the digest and confirms the on-chain record. Repo: github.com/giskard09/dapr-history-anchor

The rationale: the signature chain is tamper-evident, but its head lives in the operator's state store — nothing external pins it, so a rewound/re-signed history isn't detectable by an outside party. Anchoring the head digest on a permissionless ledger closes that specific gap (external finality) without changing anything in Dapr — it's purely additive and format-agnostic (just a 32-byte digest).

The question: is the signed `HistorySignature` (the `RawSignature` bytes) meant to be exportable/retrievable through a stable API, so an external system can obtain the chain head and verify or anchor it independently? The docs cover signing/propagation/attestation but I couldn't find guidance on exporting the signed record itself. If there's a supported path — or a reason it's intentionally internal — I'd like to understand it.

Happy to share the verifier or the exact bytes if useful. Thanks.

comments.body:
This is the right boundary to ask about. If `HistorySignature` stays internal, Dapr can prove integrity to itself; if the signed chain head is exportable, third parties can build independent finality, audit, and reputation layers without asking Dapr to become the ledger.

I would separate four objects:

- workflow identity: app/workflow/instance identity, plus signer identity
- execution history: the ordered event log Dapr already signs
- chain head: the canonical digest over the signed history at a point in time
- external anchor: optional publication of that digest somewhere outside the operator's state store

The useful API shape might be an exportable evidence envelope rather than only raw bytes:

```text
workflow_instance_id
signer_id / SVID subject
history_sequence_range
history_digest
signature_digest
signature_algorithm
created_at
verification_status
```

Then anchoring remains policy-neutral. One deployment can write the digest to Base, another to Solana, another to an internal transparency log, and another can skip anchoring entirely. Dapr only needs to make the signed evidence retrievable and stable enough that an external verifier can recompute it.

The subtle point is replay/rewind semantics. If an operator can export two valid signed heads for the same workflow instance, the verifier needs enough context to decide whether the later head extends the earlier one, forks from it, or replaces it after a rewind/continue-as-new/compaction event. So I would include sequence bounds and predecessor digest if the signing format exposes them.

From adjacent SATP / agent-receipt work, this is the same composability pattern: the runtime signs what happened, an external anchor proves when that signed state existed, and a reputation or accountability system consumes the receipt later. Keeping those layers separate lets Dapr stay runtime-neutral while still enabling cross-org verification.

Boundary note: architecture feedback only. No claim of integration, endorsement, certification, or production readiness.

Good point on replay/rewind — that was a real gap in the worked example (predecessor_signature_digest was embedded in the raw signature bytes but not exposed as a checkable field, and there was no fork scenario to test against).

Updated giskard09/dapr-history-anchor (commit 33e15cb) to close it:

- manifest.json / manifest_fork.json now expose predecessor_signature_digest_hex and sequence_range (start_event_index, event_count) as top-level fields, not just embedded in the raw bytes.
- Added a second signed head (head B) from the same genesis predecessor as the original anchored head (head A), with the terminal event rewritten (COMPLETED → FAILED) — a same-range rewrite, not an extension.
- verifier/verify.py --relation takes two anchored heads and classifies the pair as extend / fork / replace using predecessor digest + sequence range. For this pair it correctly returns "replace": same predecessor, same sequence_range, different head digest — exactly the case a signature chain alone can't distinguish from a legitimate re-sign.
- Head B anchored separately on Base mainnet (tx 0x6c46d37d63cb920bb38bc2952dd941387729abb7ae95c427adb2c32a34d735a5, block 48573841) so the classifier has two real on-chain anchors to compare, not synthetic ones. Head A's original anchor is untouched.

Same boundary as before: this doesn't imply Dapr/Diagrid integration or endorsement — worked example only, byte-exact against your public signing format.
